evidenceItems.supportsTheClaim: Teams with psychologically safe leaders report 3x higher clarity on expectations
Leaders who tolerate silence in difficult conversations achieve faster resolution
Most leaders report fleeing difficult rooms within 90 seconds

pa-evidence.thinnestEvidence: Hold the room principle relies partly on anecdotal interview data

dna-category.shelfDescription: Business / Leadership shelf, alongside titles like The Five Dysfunctions of a Team
dna-category.competitorTitle: The Five Dysfunctions of a Team by Patrick Lencioni

dna-reader.demographics: Late 30s, newly promoted to head of department, struggling to be heard by senior leadership.
dna-reader.alreadyTried: Read everything from Atomic Habits to The Mountain Is You.
dna-reader.biggestFear: Being seen as too soft to make the hard calls.
dna-reader.deepestWish: To run a department people genuinely want to work in.

dna-promise.corePromise: If you read this book, you'll learn to disappoint your team well — clearly, kindly, on purpose — and watch their respect for you grow.
dna-promise.subtitleDraft: How clear disappointment builds stronger teams

pa-thesis.thesis: Leaders fail because they optimise for being liked. The fix is learning to disappoint people clearly.
pa-thesis.thesisSentence: Most leaders fail because they optimise for being liked, not respected — and the fix is learning to disappoint people well.

principles.name: Name the gap
principles.definition: Articulate the specific difference between expectation and reality.

principles.name: Hold the room
principles.definition: Stay present in the discomfort instead of softening it.

principles.name: Ask, don't lecture
principles.definition: Surface the other person's account before offering yours.

principles.name: Decide visibly
principles.definition: Make the call where they can see you make it.

pa-framework.frameworkLogic: Sequential — each rung depends on the one below.

chapters.title: The Cost of Being Liked
chapters.job: Make the reader feel the cost of avoiding disappointment, viscerally.
chapters.readerArcEnd: Believes being liked is the obstacle.
chapters.keyEvidence: Stanford study on team trust + manager likeability divergence

sections.title: What this book will not do

figures.purpose: Illustrate the 4-step Disappointment Ladder framework showing Name the Gap, Hold the Room, Ask Don't Lecture, and Decide Visibly
figures.caption: The Disappointment Ladder — four steps to leading with clarity
figures.altText: A horizontal flow diagram with four boxes labelled: Name the Gap, Hold the Room, Ask Don't Lecture, Decide Visibly

chapters.title: Name the Gap
chapters.job: Teach the reader to articulate disappointment specifically rather than vaguely.
chapters.readerArcEnd: Has language for the specific gap.
chapters.keyEvidence: Case study of a Q3 product miss conversation

sections.title: Hook: the meeting that became a memo

genre.audience: Mid-career managers and new department heads
